text.title: PM Tool Selection — Evaluation Process and TCO Model
text.summary: Requirements gathering, MoSCoW matrix, weighted scoring, POC structure, and TCO calculation for selecting a project management tool.

rule.statement: Involve all roles in requirements gathering — PM, developer, admin, and PMO — not just power users; admins see the migration cost that power users ignore.
rule.rationale: POC participants self-select toward novelty; admin and PMO interviews surface long-tail integration and governance requirements that only appear after 3-6 months of production use.

rule.statement: Classify requirements as Must/Should/Nice (MoSCoW); designate at least one criterion as a deal-breaker (e.g., "no SAML = eliminated") to break tied scores.
rule.rationale: Weighted scorecards without deal-breakers produce close scores that rationalize any pre-made decision; a deal-breaker forces early elimination and honest evaluation.

section.title: Weighted Scoring Framework
section.p: Score tools across five weighted categories: Core Features (30%), Usability (25%), Integrations (20%), Enterprise (15%), Cost (10%). Source each score from direct testing or cited documentation — not vendor claims alone.

rule.statement: Score before the POC, then re-score after — variance reveals which criteria were assumed wrong.
rule.rationale: Pre-POC scores surface assumption gaps; post-POC scores are grounded in real use. A large variance flags criteria that were guessed rather than known.

rule.statement: Run the POC on a real epic with 8-15 issues from actual project data — not toy tickets; toy data flatters every tool.
rule.rationale: Vendor sales engineers tune POC environments to show strengths; only real data reveals workflow friction, performance under load, and integration gaps.

section.title: TCO Calculation

rule.statement: Compute 3-year TCO including direct (license), indirect (implementation, migration, training, admin), and hidden costs (productivity loss, integration development).
rule.rationale: Per-seat pricing typically represents 20-40% of year-1 TCO; migration and retraining costs dominate the first year and are systematically underestimated.

section.reference: 3-year TCO model with all cost categories

section.title: Decision and Exit Testing

rule.statement: Test the data export on day 3 of the POC — run a full data export and inspect the dump for completeness and portability.
rule.rationale: Vendor lock-in risk is only visible when you attempt to leave; discovering export limitations before commitment avoids a multi-year hostage situation.

antipattern.description: Running a POC on a decision already made by leadership.
antipattern.reason: Theater POCs erode trust when the team recognizes the outcome is predetermined; skip the process or run a genuine evaluation.

antipattern.description: Switching tools mid-project under deadline pressure.
antipattern.reason: Tool migration cost — lost automations, broken integrations, retrained muscle memory — always exceeds the friction being escaped during an active project.

section.reference: ADR template for documenting the final tool decision
